ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Steamed Chicken Noodle (Bai Zhan Ji Mian). FOODON_00002700 (chicken noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Chicken meat dish) used as closest parent; no specific code for Bai Zhan Ji Mian steamed chicken noodle exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of wheat noodles topped with sliced steamed or poached chicken, garnished with spring onion, ginger, and a light soy-sesame dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle dish — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for poached chicken (白斩鸡) and boiled wheat noodles, weighted by typical serving composition (approx. 40% chicken, 45% noodles, 15% sauce/garnish)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Foodo; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredient cross-check
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Steamed Chicken Noodle' as a combined dish. Values are estimated by compositing HPB Foodo data for white-cut chicken (bai zhan ji) and plain boiled wheat noodles in proportions typical of a Singapore hawker serving. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken breast (NDB 05064) and cooked wheat noodles (NDB 20121) used for cross-validation. Energy and macros are consistent across both sources within ±5%.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles (boiled) and poached chicken composite — analogous estimate based on GI of boiled wheat noodles (GI ~47) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Steamed Chicken Noodle (Bai Zhan Ji Mian) as a composite dish. GI of 47 is assigned based on the GI of boiled wheat noodles (GI ~47, low) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The chicken and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 63.2) / 100 ≈ 29.7, rounded to 29.8. Serving carbohydrate = 15.8 g/100g × 400 g = 63.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A standard 400 g serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced soy sauce or consume only half the dipping sauce to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of 47 but a moderate glycemic load of approximately 29.8 per serving due to the large noodle portion, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced noodle portion (200–250 g serving) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat from lean poached chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed or poached chicken is a lean protein source with low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100g), making this dish a heart-friendly option when consumed with reduced soy sauce; the absence of deep-frying limits oxidised lipid exposure relevant to cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Chicken Noodle (白斩鸡面, Bai Zhan Ji Mian) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of wheat noodles served with slices of white-cut or poached chicken, garnished with spring onion, ginger, and a light soy-sesame dipping sauce. The chicken is prepared by gentle poaching or steaming in lightly seasoned water or stock, preserving its natural juices and producing a tender, silky texture with a mild, clean flavour. The noodles are typically boiled wheat egg noodles or thin yellow noodles, served either in a light broth or dry-tossed with sesame oil and soy sauce. The dish is nutritionally balanced, providing moderate protein (~9.8 g/100g) from lean chicken, moderate carbohydrate (~15.8 g/100g) from noodles, and low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100g). Its glycemic index is low (GI ~47), driven by the wheat noodle base, though the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is moderate (~29.8). Sodium content is a clinical consideration due to soy-based seasoning. The dish is suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its lean protein and low saturated fat profile, but sodium reduction is advised for hypertensive patients. It is dairy-free and generally halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified chicken. Commonly found at Singapore hawker centres and food courts, this dish is a lighter alternative to richer noodle preparations such as char siew noodle or duck noodle.
common_user_queries: What is Bai Zhan Ji Mian?
Calories in white cut chicken noodle
Is steamed chicken noodle good for diabetics?
Sodium content of chicken noodle Singapore
Is poached chicken noodle low GI?
White cut chicken noodle vs roast duck noodle

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Steamed Chicken Noodle as a composite dish. Values are estimated by compositing HPB Singapore Foodo data for white-cut chicken and boiled wheat noodles in proportions typical of a Singapore hawker serving, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement is available in an approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on HPB Foodo entries for white-cut chicken (bai zhan ji) and boiled wheat noodles, weighted approximately 40% chicken, 45% noodles, 15% sauce/garnish by mass. Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05064 cooked chicken breast; NDB 20121 cooked wheat noodles). 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources used. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entries used for this composite. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for poached chicken and boiled wheat noodles; no added hydrogenated fats in this preparation. 6. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). No direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 7. The field dataed in the input (Hennessy Duck - Funan) is noted; the dish is a standard hawker preparation not unique to that restaurant. 8. The input cuisine_tags included 'Roasted Duck' and 'Duck Dish', which appear to be artefacts of the restaurant's primary cuisine rather than descriptors of this dish; these tags are not carried forward as they are inaccurate for Steamed Chicken Noodle.
